behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is at HTTP/raw-body boundary plus side-effect suppression. Unit tests of HMAC logic can pass while real request parsing/encoding causes mismatch handling bugs. System-level round trip verifies real 401 error contract and that no enqueue side effect occurs after an actual HTTP request.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service, send a real HTTP webhook with intentionally bad `X-Signature-256` over the raw body bytes, assert HTTP 401, then read queue storage/consumer-visible state to confirm no event was persisted/enqueued.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across external boundary and persistent side effect. Unit tests cannot prove real write-before-ack behavior. System test is needed to observe HTTP response timing against durable queue readback.
behaviors.test_description: Run service with real queue backend, issue a webhook via real HTTP, instrument timestamps around response completion and durable queue visibility, and assert queue persistence is observable before/at 200 ack emission.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The bug is module interaction/orchestration (handler vs async processor), not real network/OS behavior. No real model dependency. Rejected higher level: behavior text only requires "without waiting for downstream processing" and does not require proving external infrastructure effects beyond in-process wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + processor in-process; make processor block on a controllable promise; invoke handler and assert response is produced before releasing processor completion signal.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real concurrency, scheduler/event-loop timing, and HTTP round-trip latency under load. In-process mocks often hide contention and timing regressions. System-level wall-clock measurement is required.
behaviors.test_description: Run service instance, create concurrent load of earlier slow events plus new incoming webhook over real HTTP, measure end-to-end wall-clock for response, and assert it is <3s under configured contention scenario.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is cross-module state handoff/idempotency logic (dedupe + enqueue). No OS/network/subprocess requirement is intrinsic to this behavior, and no AI output dependency. Rejected higher level: behavior text focuses on duplicate-ID write suppression, which can be proven with integrated modules and persistent/local store readback.
behaviors.test_description: Execute two handler invocations with same event ID through real dedupe+queue modules (local persistent store allowed), then assert only one queue record exists and second path reports duplicate/no-op semantics.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This validates mapping of queue-full signal to HTTP response contract across modules. It does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior if queue-full condition is produced by integrated queue module/fake. No AI dependency. Rejected higher level: behavior text explicitly starts from "queue reports it is full," so infrastructure saturation itself is not under test here.
behaviors.test_description: Integrate handler with queue module configured to emit/full-state error, invoke request path, and assert status 503 and `Retry-After` header value/format are returned.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This is queue component behavior requiring real persistence module interactions; unit-only mocks can hide storage/constraint bugs. System not strictly required unless queue is an external service/process; local DB/filesystem-backed queue fits Integration. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with real local persistence backend and fixed capacity, enqueue up to limit, attempt one additional enqueue, and assert explicit rejection plus unchanged persisted count.
